entry.title: A Fae in Finance (How to Do Business in Fairie Book 1)
entry.summary:
by Juliet Brooks

✮✮✮✮☆

This is less a romance book than a book about going through a super traumatic experience, dealing with it, and coming out changed but good in a happily-ever-after kind of way that we usually associate with romance. There's also some romance on the side, but I didn't …
entry.content:
by Juliet Brooks

✮✮✮✮☆

This is less a romance book than a book about going through a super traumatic experience, dealing with it, and coming out changed but good in a happily-ever-after kind of way that we usually associate with romance. There's also some romance on the side, but I didn't need it. Also, interestingly, not with either of the people depicted on the cover + mentioned on the back.

The job stuff is written in a light, over-the-top way that seemed to be aiming for humorous, but it was so fucking dark.

Despite not being what I expected, I was really into this - very well written and hard to put down, even when I was mostly thinking "no really girl, we need to make a plan for murdering your boss."

entry.title: Counterweight
entry.content:
by Djuna, Anton Hur

✮✮✮✮☆

4.5 maybe - really really good, finished it in a (travel) day despite being very tired and the plot being very complicated. Delightful little meta jokes about authors and stories. A lot of people not being what they seem.

entry.title: Wash Day Diaries
entry.content:
by Jamila Rowser, Robyn Smith, Bex Glendining, Kazimir Lee

✮✮✮✮☆

Love the many-angled, many-zoom-leveled panels.

entry.title: On a Red Station, Drifting
entry.summary:
by Aliette de Bodard

✮✮✮☆☆

Wish this wasn't one of the books Overdrive mysteriously refuses to load to my ereader, reading on phone is painful. May be part of the reason I had some trouble following this - not so much the number of characters or their names, but I kept getting …
entry.content:
by Aliette de Bodard

✮✮✮☆☆

Wish this wasn't one of the books Overdrive mysteriously refuses to load to my ereader, reading on phone is painful. May be part of the reason I had some trouble following this - not so much the number of characters or their names, but I kept getting confused about the relative ages of everyone involved. Characters also seriously misjudge each other, which is great at showing the conflict between the two main women and their strong feelings of justification, but gets weird with HH, especially his last turn in the book - it doesn't hang together with either L's or Q's experiences of him. Although maybe that's the point, they also both misjudged HH and what motivates him??

entry.title: The Man in the Brown Suit (Colonel Race, #1)
entry.summary:
by Agatha Christie

no star rating

I guess I should go into a Christie novel from 1924 expecting to learn new racial slurs and new horrifying theories on the mysteries of heterosexuality, but I wasn't thinking about it very hard.

Other than all that... Eh? Safe to skip this one …
entry.content:
by Agatha Christie

no star rating

I guess I should go into a Christie novel from 1924 expecting to learn new racial slurs and new horrifying theories on the mysteries of heterosexuality, but I wasn't thinking about it very hard.

Other than all that... Eh? Safe to skip this one.

EDIT: OK, I think I'm being unfair because the last 30% of this got really full of shitty straight romance I didn't jibe with. The voice is fun, Anne as a character is fun, and it comes with the (1924) territory that she is also full of appalling opinions about colonialism and women.

entry.title: Hammajang Luck
entry.content:
by Makana Yamamoto

✮✮✮☆☆

Fun! Hard to say if I had trouble getting into it because it was lackluster or because the previous book I read happened to be too similar and better at pacing and tension.

entry.title: Dead Space

by Kali Wallace

✮✮✮✮☆

Darker and horror-ier than expected but very good. Fast read!

entry.title: Magic: A History: From Alchemy to Witchcraft, from the Ice Age to the Present
entry.summary:
by Chris Gosden

no star rating

I DID IT, I FINISHED IT. Review to come. It was higher scoring in the earlier chapters tbh, the last 2 were a slog because they read like they needed another editing pass, not typos but "ok, that sentence structure works if you read …
entry.content:
by Chris Gosden

no star rating

I DID IT, I FINISHED IT. Review to come. It was higher scoring in the earlier chapters tbh, the last 2 were a slog because they read like they needed another editing pass, not typos but "ok, that sentence structure works if you read it four times, but this one clearly changes into something else halfway through, and why did you use semicolons in that list but commas in the previous paragraph?"

Did not do what it said on the tin, which was kind of interesting as an …
entry.content:
by Diana Gabaldon, Jeff Woodman

✮✮✮☆☆

Brigadier Lethbridge-Stewart mention, obviously. Sam says only I get how many Doctor Who references there are in her books - MAYBE IT'S JUST ME, IDK, BUT I DON'T THINK SO.

Did not do what it said on the tin, which was kind of interesting as an authorial choice. I'm not mad about it.

entry.title: The Transgender Issue: An Argument for Justice
entry.content:
by Shon Faye

✮✮✮✮☆

Oops, I finished this as an audiobook late last year sometime. Not painful to listen to despite being a summation of stuff I already knew; I think I mainly wanted to know how recommendable this was, and the answer is: very.

by Tillie Walden

by Shola von Reinhold

I think I never wrote a review because I need a book club, or possibly a grad student seminar, about this one. Will report back if/when I convince someone else to read it and yell about it with me.

entry.title: Shubeik Lubeik
entry.summary:
by Deena Mohamed

✮✮✮✮✮

Rebecca G strong rec!

Rebecca was sooo right. Does that "treat a fantasy concept as sci-fi" thing I love so much, with an international NGO bent to it - the informational fact sheets between the chapters were just chef's kiss. Rebecca surely appreciated these even more because it's …
entry.content:
by Deena Mohamed

✮✮✮✮✮

Rebecca G strong rec!

Rebecca was sooo right. Does that "treat a fantasy concept as sci-fi" thing I love so much, with an international NGO bent to it - the informational fact sheets between the chapters were just chef's kiss. Rebecca surely appreciated these even more because it's her field, but I got a kick out of 'em.

I was terribly afraid that it was going to be a "be careful what you wish for" theme from the way the first story starts, but it's NOT. So if that's also not your thing, keep going!

entry.title: The Mountains of Mourning (Vorkosigan Saga, #5.1)
entry.summary:
by Lois McMaster Bujold

✮✮✮✮☆

Not my first read, just recording for the first time. Is punishment the only way to show that a class of people matters and that crime against them is wrong? Wondering how abolitionists think about this - maybe it's a "master's tools, master's house" idea. Also the …
entry.content:
by Lois McMaster Bujold

✮✮✮✮☆

Not my first read, just recording for the first time. Is punishment the only way to show that a class of people matters and that crime against them is wrong? Wondering how abolitionists think about this - maybe it's a "master's tools, master's house" idea. Also the idea that it's only when a society is wealthy enough can people who might not be able to contribute become less of a threat, although tbh I think that might not reflect reality, sort of like how hunter-gatherers have more leisure than farmers even though farmers think the opposite.

...come to think of it, what DO the hill people do to subsist? It's not really clear in the story. Are they growing poppies for opium??? Main lady picks berries at one point; people having lots of children implies farmers perhaps.

entry.summary:
by Emerson Whitney

by Emerson Whitney

✮✮✮☆☆

I liked it better than [b:Time Is the Thing a Body Moves Through|42372517|Time Is the Thing a Body Moves Through|T. Fleischmann|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1603210944l/42372517.SY75.jpg|66029276] or [b:Socialist Realism|42372520|Socialist Realism|Trisha Low|https://i.gr-assets.com/images/S/compressed.photo.goodreads.com/books/1552173675l/42372520.SX50.jpg|66029279] which other people whose opinions I respect liked more. Maybe this is more willing to tell you what it's about, which I appreciate. It was good. Not a new favorite or anything though.

entry.title: Quantum Moon
entry.summary:
by Denise Vitola

no star rating

Page 146 - I have never had a book go from "not bad" to JESUS CHRIST WHAT THE FUCK in the space of one sentence like this, I think I have whiplash

Review to come I guess, like basically it wasn't bad and did what …
entry.content:
by Denise Vitola

no star rating

Page 146 - I have never had a book go from "not bad" to JESUS CHRIST WHAT THE FUCK in the space of one sentence like this, I think I have whiplash

Review to come I guess, like basically it wasn't bad and did what it said on the tin and had a few interesting ideas, but the sudden het romance was both completely bananas and completely unnecessary to the plot.

The only physical characteristics that are described are 1. Ty's view of her own body changes (important to the plot), 2. Gibson has abs (why???????), 3. Fat people being gross and fat and gross, 4. Dirty people having a fairly unbelievable amount of grease coming out of their hair with every movement

entry.title: The 7½ Deaths of Evelyn Hardcastle
entry.summary:
by Stuart Turton

✮✮☆☆☆

Kinda dumb book that thinks it is exceedingly smart. I can and do enjoy dumb books that do what they say on the tin, but I got more and more annoyed as I went on in this one because Turton started getting more and more philosophical and …
entry.content:
by Stuart Turton

✮✮☆☆☆

Kinda dumb book that thinks it is exceedingly smart. I can and do enjoy dumb books that do what they say on the tin, but I got more and more annoyed as I went on in this one because Turton started getting more and more philosophical and sentimental, and it was just not as profound as he thought it was.

I love time travel shenanigans, so that was all right. I fucking hate body swapping as a concept, but I was ok with it here, and I think it's because a lot of the authorial choices take away the horror of it. They're none of them real, Aiden can't hurt them. Also does the thing that every body-swap book has to do and has one of the bodies be super fat and that's the horror, literally every paragraph for 70 pages contains a reference to how horrifying and disgusting and pathetic and disabling the body is. The speculative/fantasy parts were kind of a flop, I didn't get any sense of the society that would have created them, they were just convenient for telling the story Turton wanted to tell.

Forty thousand twists!

entry.title: Tommy Cabot Was Here (The Cabots, #1)
entry.content:
by Cat Sebastian

✮✮✮☆☆

Miscommunication as a conflict but it actually kinda makes sense and works

...

This took me SO LONG to finish. Longer review later, but I do have to say that I feel misled by all the quotes on the cover making …
entry.content:
by Andrea Lawlor

✮✮✮☆☆

started and not finished 2022-11-04 i'll be back, i just wanted goodreads' database to stop bugging me about it

...

This took me SO LONG to finish. Longer review later, but I do have to say that I feel misled by all the quotes on the cover making this out to be omg so edgy and smutty. It was good and Paul is obsessed with cruising but it really wasn't particularly risque. ...IDK maybe it was just the "difficult to quote in a family newspaper" one that irked me most deeply, it's not like there's nothing but coast-to-coast orgy in here.

I also enjoyed the introductory notes about how this is reclaiming the word PORNOGRAPHY, because having a plot and being a …
entry.content:
by Iris Jay, Nero O'Reilly

✮✮✮✮✮

Beautiful. Interesting. Hot.

Edit: Actually I'm giving it an extra star because of just how fucking beautiful the art is. Absolutely cannot be overstated.

I also enjoyed the introductory notes about how this is reclaiming the word PORNOGRAPHY, because having a plot and being a work of art does not make it something more classy/euphemistic like "erotica."

entry.title: Summer Fun
entry.content:
by Jeanne Thornton

no star rating

Don't know how to rate that - fuck if it got depressing towards the end. I don't know jack about the Beach Boys. Very well done though, far as I can tell.

entry.title: The Saint of Bright Doors
entry.summary:
by Vajra Chandrasekera

✮✮✮✮✮

5 stars because I’m blown away by the complexity and what Chandrasekera has achieved. As exciting and original as Ancillary Justice when I first read it.

  • I did not find this slow paced, as others have said, but it may have been because I was reading …
entry.content:
by Vajra Chandrasekera

✮✮✮✮✮

5 stars because I’m blown away by the complexity and what Chandrasekera has achieved. As exciting and original as Ancillary Justice when I first read it.

  • I did not find this slow paced, as others have said, but it may have been because I was reading it so fast. I was deeply immersed and found it very hard to put down.

  • I was willing to give the worldbuilding some grace and not question its roiling mix of magic, mythology, technology, and 21st century terms and concepts because I was so interested in the plot. That paid off in a big way!!

  • This goes hard on Buddhism, in a way that feels daring and dangerous if the world of this book mirrors the religious politics of Sri Lanka in any serious way. My intro-level reading on Buddhism last year was invaluable even though it was clear I was missing a lot. Some concepts and stories I was able to pick out: Vido=Ananda, pure land and buddha fields (probably, I still don’t really grok these), the Mahayana/Theravada split in its outlines.

  • Queer and trans, anticolonial, antiracist and anti-casteist, and overall quite anarchist, but not in a way that's idealistic about any of those things. Doesn't put any identities (or people) on a pedestal. Not idealistic about human nature nor the way humans relate to each other at all, but somehow still believes in trying and hope and revolution, even without believing that we’ll ever eliminate violence.

  • Being in my mid-late 30s and really resonating with the scene where Fetter is thinking about the difference between him and Caduv, in their 20s and still forming their own identities and selfhoods, and the contrast with Koel's full fledged adulthood, which is aiming to form the world.

  • I really really really fuck with the incredible surreal Kafkaesque journey through death and out the other side depicted as a refugee/internment camp.

  • The end of the story was sudden and frustrating in some ways but it also worked really well: if a chosen one is the person a story is told about, then the true end of their chosenness is the end of them being seen/surveilled by the story being told about them. It’s also landed as a much happier ending than I feared: Fetter finally gets to be free and himself and alive, shaped by his family and his history, like we all are, but no longer controlled by them. Like his family, the reader is forced to realize that we can no longer completely understand Fetter and his life.

  • I was so sure after the pyre that Fetter was the Man in the Fire - I think I like even more that he doesn't seem to be, though!

Original review: Extremely hard to put down, now I can finally do household chores.

entry.title: The Bullet That Missed (Thursday Murder Club, #3)
entry.summary:
by Richard Osman

✮✮✮☆☆

I found this one a bit over the top compared to the previous two, and a bit less funny. The more pervasive deviations from plausibility make the really wild moments less unexpected/surprising. Is there a technical word for that, in comedy?

The Connie and Ibrahim conversations …
entry.content:
by Richard Osman

✮✮✮☆☆

I found this one a bit over the top compared to the previous two, and a bit less funny. The more pervasive deviations from plausibility make the really wild moments less unexpected/surprising. Is there a technical word for that, in comedy?

The Connie and Ibrahim conversations were very well done, and Bogdan and Stephen. (Although dementia without changes to the person's emotional regulation abilities.... idk, that's a specific kind of sadness that avoids a much bigger and more difficult sadness.) The Viking's whole storyline and pretty much every scene with him in it felt forced, though I can't point to anything specifically wrong with it.

entry.title: Heavyweight: A Family Story of the Holocaust, Empire, and Memory
entry.summary:
by Solomon J. Brager

✮✮✮✮☆

(Short review because I don't wanna forget to review, maybe more later!)

Really really really really really good and necessary. The connection between German colonial violence abroad and how it got brought home is just---I never have learned about that anywhere else, and it makes sense …
entry.content:
by Solomon J. Brager

✮✮✮✮☆

(Short review because I don't wanna forget to review, maybe more later!)

Really really really really really good and necessary. The connection between German colonial violence abroad and how it got brought home is just---I never have learned about that anywhere else, and it makes sense of a lot of things??

The star off is mostly because of some layout and formatting issues. On a handful of pages I couldn't figure out in what order to read the panels, or which words went with which panel, which is one of the most basic things an editor should be flagging. It's also not as high resolution as a frequent graphic novel reader would expect, and there was something a little weird about the margins, like this was a text book that happened to just have images on every page - I have to look back at it.

entry.title: The Musgrave Ritual - a Sherlock Holmes Short Story (The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, #5)
entry.summary:
by Arthur Conan Doyle

✮✮✮☆☆

Always interesting to see in action the British tradition of being wildly racist about people both outside and inside their tiny islands. Where else other than Conan-Doyle and Sayers can you find main characters calling the Welsh and Scottish feral impulsive savages who can't talk right …
entry.content:
by Arthur Conan Doyle

✮✮✮☆☆

Always interesting to see in action the British tradition of being wildly racist about people both outside and inside their tiny islands. Where else other than Conan-Doyle and Sayers can you find main characters calling the Welsh and Scottish feral impulsive savages who can't talk right?

entry.title: Gathering Moss: A Natural and Cultural History of Mosses
entry.summary:
by Robin Wall Kimmerer

✮✮✮✮✮

What an incredibly appropriate book to read while in Vancouver BC, home of mosses on every surface that can't move fast enough, and a place where settlers have en masse started (emphasis on started) thinking about what it would be like to acknowledge what they've (we've …
entry.content:
by Robin Wall Kimmerer

✮✮✮✮✮

What an incredibly appropriate book to read while in Vancouver BC, home of mosses on every surface that can't move fast enough, and a place where settlers have en masse started (emphasis on started) thinking about what it would be like to acknowledge what they've (we've) done to get where they (we) are. Spent a lot of time thinking about what land acknowledgements can and can't do - literally performative and try-hard without changing anything concrete, yes, of course, but I now think there is actually something valuable in drawing attention repeatedly - eventually you start wondering why your actions don't match your words, and then you have to start taking action.

I am predictably now obsessed with mosses and will be seeking out some of the books in the further reading/bibliography, because of course I am.

entry.title: Lord John and the Private Matter (Lord John Grey, #1)
entry.summary:
by Diana Gabaldon

✮✮✮✮☆

Airplane read the other weekend, absolutely hoovered it up. I only know enough queer history to be on high alert anytime someone tries to write historical gayness, but I don't know enough to say that she got anything wrong - this seems heavily grounded in historical research, so …
entry.content:
by Diana Gabaldon

✮✮✮✮☆

Airplane read the other weekend, absolutely hoovered it up. I only know enough queer history to be on high alert anytime someone tries to write historical gayness, but I don't know enough to say that she got anything wrong - this seems heavily grounded in historical research, so it's probably a dinosaurs-without-feathers thing at worst.

I'm still not ever planning to read or watch Outlander, but I was delighted to see that this is a series! Desperate to find out WTF happened at the Hellfire Club, just like whatshisface.

(I cannot think about Outlander at all ever without sharing my favorite fact, that it's Second!Doctor companion fanfic with the serial numbers filed off; we all know that right? That Jamie Fraser = Jamie McCrimmon the highlander + Frazer Hines the actor who portrayed him? OK, good.)

entry.title: Jade City (The Green Bone Saga, #1)
entry.summary:
by Fonda Lee

✮✮✮☆☆

Took a while (100+ pages iirc) to get into, but then it moved well. Great intricate plot, really well done political conflict and characterizations. The thought of reading another 1000 pages to continue the story makes me tired, though, so I'm probably not going to.

The character …
entry.content:
by Fonda Lee

✮✮✮☆☆

Took a while (100+ pages iirc) to get into, but then it moved well. Great intricate plot, really well done political conflict and characterizations. The thought of reading another 1000 pages to continue the story makes me tired, though, so I'm probably not going to.

The character work was the most impressive tbh - how many times are you told a character is charismatic? But scenes like the one between Hilo and one of the chief politicians, where they don't like each other but Hilo brings him around by telling him why it's in his interest and then seals it by making a comment about how funny it is that even though they hate everything about each other they're going to be besties if they survive -- damn. You can actually see how that kind of relationship is built. Characters repeatedly brought me around to seeing their point of view even as I didn't like or agree with them.

Much of the book is male dominated (maybe one of the reasons it took me a while to get into it at the start), but eventually several female characters become more central and get more POV chapters, and there's one queer person so far.

entry.title: The Pain Scale
entry.content:
by Eula Biss

✮✮✮✮✮

Read online here: https://www.snreview.org/biss.pdf

entry.title: The Collected Schizophrenias: Essays
entry.summary:
by Esmé Weijun Wang

✮✮✮✮☆

I've been looking for a few years for first-person accounts of psychosis; there really aren't many. (#mentalhealthawareness indeed.) I thought this was very good---very well written, very thoughtful, very honest, very affecting---though of course I'm saying that from the outside.

The smattering of reviews that don't …
entry.content:
by Esmé Weijun Wang

✮✮✮✮☆

I've been looking for a few years for first-person accounts of psychosis; there really aren't many. (#mentalhealthawareness indeed.) I thought this was very good---very well written, very thoughtful, very honest, very affecting---though of course I'm saying that from the outside.

The smattering of reviews that don't like this book because Wang is "too put together" and "too privileged" are bugging me. (Maybe I don't read enough cis white woman anxiety-and-depression memoirs to see if people are making the same "insufficiently radical!" claims in the reviews, though.) I didn't get the impression Wang was trying to speak for anyone but herself when she was talking about her experiences of masking. She's showing some of her uglier moments instead of uttering radical platitudes, and I appreciate it.

entry.title: Babel
entry.summary:
by R.F. Kuang

✮✮✮✮☆

Really really good and worthwhile. 4.5 but rounded down because it's impressive but didn't blow me out of the water emotionally and because the one-off interlude chapters from Letty and Victoire felt very pastede on and tell-heavy.

Actually, I'm a big fan of footnotes (nonlinear …
entry.content:
by R.F. Kuang

✮✮✮✮☆

Really really good and worthwhile. 4.5 but rounded down because it's impressive but didn't blow me out of the water emotionally and because the one-off interlude chapters from Letty and Victoire felt very pastede on and tell-heavy.

Actually, I'm a big fan of footnotes (nonlinear ftw!) but footnotes shouldn't stop the momentum of the scene or spell out things that the story is trying to show. Also, the chapter that's just the newspaper article footnote was a devastatingly good idea for how to deliver that plot point/bit of emotion, but not well executed - I've read 1800s newspapers, that is NOT how they talk nor who they talk to, not even close - and it brought me out of the story.

The ideas, they're all there, and they're worth it. The structure of the story itself is really really good. The writing is the weak link I think - mostly up to the task, but occasionally fumbles.

entry.title: The Man Who Died Twice (Thursday Murder Club, #2)
entry.content:
by Richard Osman

✮✮✮✮☆

entry.title: A Nun in the Closet
entry.summary:
by Dorothy Gilman

✮✮✮☆☆

Insubstantial but feel-good (in a 1975 way) mystery novel borrowed from Sarah. I had a good time! Sister John would be much more openly butch if I was Gilman's editor. (She named herself Sister John! Like, that is her first and only name!! And her favorite assignment …
entry.content:
by Dorothy Gilman

✮✮✮☆☆

Insubstantial but feel-good (in a 1975 way) mystery novel borrowed from Sarah. I had a good time! Sister John would be much more openly butch if I was Gilman's editor. (She named herself Sister John! Like, that is her first and only name!! And her favorite assignment is fixing the printing press!!! OK then!!!!) Sister Hyacinthe also has big dyke energy with the herbs and the going barefoot.

The (fabulous) cover is unfortunately false advertising - neither of the nuns learns to ride a motorcycle. :( It's also not gay, but it's not hard to get there either.

entry.title: Side Affects: On Being Trans and Feeling Bad
entry.content:
by Hil Malatino

✮✮✮✮☆

entry.title: Paladin's Strength (The Saint of Steel, #2)
entry.summary:
by T. Kingfisher

✮✮✮☆☆

A little disappointed on the romance front tbh! I thought Istvhan's relationship with Beartongue from the first book was going to be the focus, or at least get a lot more page time, but no, Istvhan/Clara is basically a repeat of the last book's couple. (Clara's …
entry.content:
by T. Kingfisher

✮✮✮☆☆

A little disappointed on the romance front tbh! I thought Istvhan's relationship with Beartongue from the first book was going to be the focus, or at least get a lot more page time, but no, Istvhan/Clara is basically a repeat of the last book's couple. (Clara's huge chest and her attitude about it made me close the book and offer up a moment of silent gratitude for general anesthesia and top surgery!!!) This is a purely functional romance novel - certain tropes expected and delivered. I liked Clara and Istvhan fine, but Beartongue is way more interesting.

Plot was again enough to keep me interested and was a nice vacation from my brain. There was a lot of killing guards and other NPCs and not really caring, which is not my favorite thing, but is realistic for the world (D&D campaign fantasy with more interest in local politics) and the kinds of people Istvhan and Clara are.
